Because people prefer to follow the recommendations of true experts, you'll want to communicate your relevant knowledge and experience before you begin any influence attempt. But that's not as straightforward as it sounds, especially in face-to-face interactions. Even if you are a genuine authority, it would be a mistake to try to present your credentials yourself. Research shows you'll come off as boastful, which will cause observers to light and trust you less. Let's say you're making a presentation to a group that is unfamiliar with you, rather than introducing yourself and your credentials, arrange for someone from the group, preferably a leader, to do the introduction for you. Beforehand, simply provide that individual with a brief list of the points that show your expertise on the topic. That way, people in the meeting will be honestly informed of your qualifications. They don't have a crystal ball, after all. You won't have to look like a braggart in the process. Alternatively, you can send the leader a copy of your LinkedIn, resume, or your bio to distribute to meeting members before your presentation. Now, if your meeting is with just one or two individuals, send a letter of introduction to each that says, I'm looking forward to our meeting on Wednesday on the topic of X. My background and experience on X are described in the attached bio. The T-Incite is never to begin a face-to-face meeting with a recitation of your positive qualities. You can't come in and say, before we begin, let me just tell you how great. I really am. It goes against all the norms of proper and social interaction, and you'll lose influenced points as a shameless self-promoter. So, the positive information has to be transmitted through another person or in written form ahead of the meeting.
